overall.summary: The chapter quiz set is solid overall: questions are generally grounded in the text, aligned with the chapter's foundational position, and mostly test systems reasoning rather than rote recall. The remaining issues are concentrated in a handful of FILL items, one weak TF, some overly generic stems/LOs, and a small number of questions that unnecessarily re-explain prior-chapter concepts instead of building on them.

missed_buildup_opportunities.explanation: The section rationale integrates prior context well, but this quiz set rarely leverages prior-chapter vocabulary directly. This question set could have assumed the reader already knows the AI Triad and used it more explicitly in stems or answers instead of re-centering the lifecycle in isolation.

The question correctly applies a prior-chapter concept, but the answer partially re-explains the iron-law mapping in a way that pads the item. Because the iron law was introduced earlier, the item could be more compact and application-focused.
This item is valid, but it reintroduces the iron-law framing at a fairly basic level instead of assuming familiarity and pushing the reader into a richer application scenario.

Like the analogous data-collection item, this question uses a prior-chapter term correctly but at a definitional level. At chapter 3, the audit bar should favor applying prior concepts rather than repeatedly asking which term a stage 'primarily defines.'

This is another structurally similar iron-law mapping item. The chapter is still foundational, but the buildup opportunity is to use the prior concept in a concrete deployment trade-off rather than re-asking its stage-to-term correspondence.

build_up.chapter_level_assessment: The quizzes respect the no-forward-reference rule and do use earlier concepts like the AI Triad, deployment paradigms, and the iron law. However, the chapter sometimes feels slightly self-contained rather than fully built on prior context, especially in several iron-law mapping questions that revisit prior concepts at a near-definitional level instead of using them more naturally.

distribution.type_mix_assessment: slightly over-MCQ and under-ORDER, but acceptable for a single chapter; SHORT presence is healthy, FILL/TF are within reason

per_question_issues.description: The stem 'Which description best captures...' is somewhat templated and generic. The question is still valid, but this phrasing appears in many generated quiz sets and weakens chapter-specific voice.
per_question_issues.suggested_fix: Replace with a more concrete scenario stem, such as contrasting a team that stops at deployment with one that retrains after drift, and ask which view matches the chapter's lifecycle definition.

per_question_issues.description: The FILL answer 'contract' is close to pure term recall and does not require much conceptual inference once the phrase 'inputs, expected deliverables, and quality invariants' is given. It risks testing naming rather than systems reasoning.
per_question_issues.suggested_fix: Convert this into a SHORT or MCQ about why treating stage boundaries as contracts catches errors earlier, or make the blank require inference from a scenario about a blocked stage transition.

per_question_issues.description: The TF claim is fairly easy because the section repeatedly emphasizes that scaling changes constraints, fairness targets, and hardware assumptions. The wrong answer is not obvious from grammar, but it is strongly signposted by the section.
per_question_issues.suggested_fix: Make the statement more misconception-shaped, e.g. contrast stable business goals with evolving subgroup thresholds or hardware constraints, so the reader must reason about what changes and what remains fixed.

per_question_issues.description: This question mostly asks the reader to remember which iron-law term data collection maps to. That is more classificatory recall than systems reasoning, especially since similar stage-to-term questions recur in multiple sections.
per_question_issues.suggested_fix: Replace it with a scenario asking how a data-collection choice changes D_vol or bandwidth pressure in a concrete deployment, requiring application instead of term matching.

per_question_issues.description: This is another stage-to-iron-law mapping question whose main demand is identifying the named term O. In aggregate with similar items elsewhere, it becomes formulaic and somewhat repetitive.
per_question_issues.suggested_fix: Ask the reader to compare two architecture choices and infer which one raises the Operations term and why that matters for the edge-constrained DR workflow.

per_question_issues.description: The FILL item for 'lineage' is useful conceptually but still leans too heavily on term recovery from a near-definition. It is one of the weaker items in an otherwise strong section.
per_question_issues.suggested_fix: Rewrite as a SHORT asking why lack of lineage makes a two-percent regression expensive to diagnose, or an MCQ comparing lineage with checkpoints, seeds, and benchmarks.

per_question_issues.description: This question asks for the deployment stage's primary iron-law objective, which is basically another direct mapping exercise. It is valid but repetitive given similar items in data collection, model development, and lifecycle stages.
per_question_issues.suggested_fix: Turn it into a concrete deployment scenario involving latency, integration overhead, and offline constraints, then ask which engineering action best reduces the overhead term.

per_question_issues.description: The 'data lineage' blank is another term-recall item. The surrounding sentence narrows the answer heavily, so the conceptual work is minimal.
per_question_issues.suggested_fix: Ask instead how lineage changes root-cause analysis time when one clinic's accuracy drops, forcing the reader to reason about why metadata trails matter operationally.

per_question_issues.description: Although conceptually sound, this item again asks for the named principle's core claim in a fairly direct way. As a capstone section, it could push harder on application or trade-off reasoning instead of principle identification.
per_question_issues.suggested_fix: Embed the principle in a concrete late-discovery scenario and ask which kind of rework pattern it predicts, rather than naming the principle outright.

per_question_issues.description: This FILL item is almost pure name recall of 'Constraint Propagation Principle.' Because the summary already revisits the concept directly, the answer is highly cued and not very diagnostic.
per_question_issues.suggested_fix: Replace with a SHORT asking how the 2^(N-1) pattern should change early project planning, or an MCQ contrasting it with a linear cost-growth assumption.
